column1: **An unsupervised anomaly-detection pipeline that flags high-risk insurance claims for investigation
column3: and produces an agent-level risk scorecard for internal audit.**

column1: Insurance carriers process thousands of claims with no reliable way to know
column3: which ones deserve a closer look. Manual audit teams have limited capacity — they can't review every claim
column4: so they need a way to **rank** claims by risk and **quantify** how much dollar exposure sits in the highest-risk tier
column5: rather than a simple thumbs up / thumbs down per claim.

column1: This project simulates that workflow end-to-end: ingest claim
column3: and vendor records → engineer red-flag features → score every claim with two independent unsupervised models → roll the results up into a business-usable risk report.

column1: The Pareto chart below plots cumulative dollar value captured as you review claims in risk-score order. A model that mainly targets *big* claims would bow sharply above the diagonal early on. It doesn't:

This says something specific: **the model is not simply a large-claims detector.** Reviewing the top 5% of claims by risk score captures roughly 5% of total dollar value — no better than reviewing a random 5%. It's catching claims that are *structurally unusual* (odd combinations of timing
column3: relationships) rather than claims that are *large*. For an audit team
column5: not a bug — a large-claims filter is trivial to build without any of this; the value of this pipeline is in the pattern-based signal
column6: not dollar-sorting. It's called out here rather than glossed over because a resume-ready analysis should say what a model *doesn't* do as clearly as what it does.

column1: ## 5. Bugs Fixed From the Original Notebook
This project started as a Colab notebook that crashed on a relative file path. Beyond that
column2: two silent (non-crashing) issues were found and fixed:

column1: 1. **The bank-account collusion rule never fired** — 0 out of 10
column3: on the original single feature. It's kept
column4: but is no longer the *only* collusion signal; address-match and surname-match were added and both fire on real (if rare) cases.

column1: 2. **Vendor postal code was merged into the dataset but never used** — it was joined in
column2: then dropped without ever being compared to anything. Two new features (`AGENT_VENDOR_SAME_ZIP`
column3: `CUSTOMER_VENDOR_SAME_ZIP`) now use it.

column1: Also fixed: no random seed (results weren't reproducible run-to-run)
column2: the autoencoder was trained and scored on the exact same rows with no held-out portion
column3: and skewed monetary features went into a linear scaler untransformed.

column1: - **No ground-truth fraud label exists in this dataset.** Model agreement is a reasonable substitute for validation
column2: not a replacement for it. In production
column3: flagged claims would need a feedback loop (confirmed fraud / false positive) to actually measure precision.

column1: - **Synthetic data.** Distributions and correlations were generated
